concerns.text: The plan proposes a one-sample Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) test against Exponential(λ=1) on data normalized by its own mean (implicitly, as the mean of Δ/log p is ~1). This violates the independence assumption of the KS test. When parameters are estimated from the data (even if forced to 1.0 by normalization), the null distribution of the KS statistic changes, inflating p-values and reducing power to detect deviations. The plan acknowledges this as a 'caveat' but does not propose a corrected methodology (e.g., Lilliefors test, parametric bootstrap, or testing against a distribution with a free scale parameter).

The plan uses a one-sample t-test comparing window means to 1.0. However, the normalized gaps are not independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) in the way required for a standard t-test; they exhibit autocorrelation and the variance of the normalized gap is not constant across the range (heteroscedasticity). Furthermore, the 'theoretical mean' of 1.0 is derived from the same normalization applied to the data, creating a circular reference. A more robust approach would be to compare the empirical distribution in the window against the global empirical distribution (e.g., via a two-sample KS test or Anderson-Darling) rather than a fixed theoretical constant.

The definition of 'gap' as the distance between the *starts* of consecutive twin prime pairs (p_{n+1} - p_n) conflates the gap between twin primes with the gap between *occurrences* of twin primes. The Cramér model and Hardy-Littlewood conjectures typically apply to the distribution of gaps between *consecutive primes* or the distribution of the *twin prime constant* itself. The specific distribution of gaps between *twin prime pairs* (which are sparse and clustered) may not follow the standard exponential law for prime gaps. The null hypothesis (Exponential(1)) may be theoretically unsound for this specific derived metric without a specific citation supporting the exponential distribution of *inter-twin-prime-pair* gaps.
